title: Pyrography Christmas tree ornaments
subtitle: Burned decoration on small birch rounds for the Christmas tree
excerpt: Small birch branch cross-cuts, 40 to 60 mm in diameter and 10 mm thick, decorated with simple pyrography designs and hung on the Christmas tree. Each ornament takes fifteen minutes once the blanks are prepared. The small scale makes them a good first pyrography project: the design areas are tight and errors are small.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the birch round preparation; general pyrography technique from traditional decorative woodcraft traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of decorating wood by burning a design into the surface with a heated metal tip.
A fine-pointed pyrography tip for line work and detail. The main tip for ornament work at small scale.
The concentric growth rings visible on a cross-cut wood surface. On ornament-sized birch rounds, the rings are closely spaced and fine.

content.text: Birch branch cross-cuts show fine, closely spaced
that make a natural background for
ornaments. The ring pattern supports simple designs well. A batch of ten ornaments from a 600 mm section of birch branch takes about two hours.

Slice the birch branch into 10 mm rounds with the tenon saw or a hand mitre saw. Sand both faces of each round to 320-grit. The top face is the design face; sand it to a smooth finish that the writing tip can travel over without catching saw marks. Bore a 3 mm hanging hole 6 mm in from the top edge, centred on the diameter, before burning: drilling after burning would risk flaking the burned design.

The most reliable first designs for a 50 mm round are: a simple star or snowflake drawn from the centre pith outward; a ring of dots at the outer annual ring; initials or a short word in the centre; or a simple botanical silhouette (leaf, holly sprig). Sketch the design in pencil before burning. On this small scale, the pencil line is only a guide; the final burned line will be drawn freehand over it.

at medium heat. Rest the little finger of the burning hand against the bench edge for stability. Work outward from the centre of the design to the edge. Clean the tip on the cooling stand every four to five ornaments; carbon builds up quickly on small, repetitive work. The small scale means mistakes are proportionally small; a stray line on a 50 mm ornament reads as texture rather than an error from a normal viewing distance.

Blow off all carbon dust. Thread a 150 mm length of natural twine through the hanging hole and tie a loop. No finish is needed for Christmas ornaments; the raw birch surface with its bark edge gives a natural look that is part of the appeal. If the bark edge splits or loosens slightly during drying, this is normal and does not need correction.
Named ornaments: a name and year make a personalised gift or an annual family ornament tradition.
Apple wood rounds: apple branch gives a finer annual ring pattern and burns to a warmer amber than birch.
Gift tags: a smaller slice 30 mm across with a simple border makes a permanent gift tag that can be kept as a keepsake.
